title: Evaluating AI Use Cases
description: Learn a practical framework for identifying, evaluating, and scoping AI use cases — whether you're a product manager building a roadmap, a designer prototyping experiences, or a business leader evaluating AI investments.
learningObjectives: Apply a structured framework to evaluate whether AI is the right solution for a problem
Distinguish between high-value and low-value AI use cases
Define success metrics and scope for an AI-powered feature

sections.title: Finding the Right Problems for AI
sections.content:
Not every problem benefits from AI. The most common mistake organizations make is starting with the technology ("let's use AI!") rather than the problem ("this process is slow and error-prone — could AI help?"). Choosing the right problem is the single most important decision in any AI initiative, and it deserves careful thought before anyone writes a line of code.

AI excels at tasks that revolve around language and structured reasoning. **Language understanding** is one of its core strengths — interpreting unstructured text, answering questions about documents, and extracting meaning from messy inputs that would take humans considerable time to process. Closely related is **generation with guardrails**, where AI drafts content, writes code, or produces variations within defined constraints. These generative tasks benefit enormously from AI's ability to produce fluent, context-aware output at scale.

Beyond generation, AI is particularly effective at **classification and routing** — sorting items into categories, prioritizing queues, and directing requests to the right team or workflow. It also handles **transformation** tasks well, such as converting between formats, summarizing lengthy documents, translating across languages, and restructuring data into new shapes. Finally, AI brings real capability to **analysis and reasoning**, where it can compare options, identify patterns across text, and evaluate information against a set of criteria.

On the other hand, certain categories of tasks remain a poor fit. When a task demands **deterministic correctness** — where even a 1% error rate is unacceptable, as in financial calculations or compliance checks — AI alone will not be sufficient. Tasks requiring **real-time data** also present challenges, because AI does not have access to live information unless it is connected to external tools. Similarly, AI lacks **institutional memory**; it cannot recall past conversations or organizational history without a retrieval system feeding it that context. And of course, **physical world interaction** falls outside AI's reach entirely — it processes text and images, not physical objects.

The sweet spot for AI is tasks that humans can do but that are time-consuming, repetitive, or don't scale — and where the occasional imperfect output is acceptable or can be caught by review.

sections.title: The AI Use Case Evaluation Framework
sections.content:
When evaluating a potential AI use case, it helps to score it across five dimensions. These dimensions work together to give you a balanced picture — a use case might score highly on value but poorly on risk, and that tradeoff is exactly what you need to understand before investing.

**1. Value — How much impact does this have?**
Estimate the time saved, quality improved, or new capability unlocked. A use case that saves 10 hours per week for a team of 20 is worth more than one that saves 5 minutes per day for one person. Think beyond simple time savings, too — sometimes the real value is enabling something that was previously impossible, like offering personalized responses to every customer inquiry instead of relying on generic templates.

**2. Feasibility — Can AI actually do this well?**
The best way to gauge feasibility is to test the core task with Claude directly. If Claude can handle 80% or more of cases correctly with a well-crafted prompt, the use case is feasible. If it struggles below 60%, the task may need a fundamentally different approach or will require heavy human oversight that erodes the value proposition. This quick prototyping step can save months of wasted development effort.

**3. Risk — What happens when AI gets it wrong?**
Consider the blast radius of errors. An AI that miscategorizes an internal support ticket has low risk — someone notices and recategorizes it. An AI that sends incorrect medical information has extremely high risk, with potentially serious consequences. Higher risk demands more guardrails, human review steps, or a hybrid approach where AI assists rather than acts autonomously.

**4. Data readiness — Do you have what you need?**
AI needs context to work well. The key questions here are whether you have the documents, examples, or data that Claude would need to perform the task, whether that data is clean and accessible, and whether it is representative of the full range of inputs the system will encounter. Missing or poor-quality data is the most common blocker for AI projects, so assessing this dimension early prevents costly surprises later.

**5. User acceptance — Will people actually use it?**
The best AI feature fails if users don't trust it or find the interaction awkward. Consider whether the target users are comfortable with AI tools, whether the interaction model feels natural within their existing workflow, and whether there's a graceful fallback when AI isn't helpful. Forcing AI into a workflow where users are skeptical often leads to low adoption regardless of the technology's actual quality.

A quick scoring approach: rate each dimension 1-5 and multiply. A score above 2,000 (out of 3,125) suggests a strong use case. Below 500 suggests significant challenges.

sections.title: Scoping and Measuring Success
sections.content:
Once you've identified a promising use case, scope it tightly before building. The most successful AI features start narrow and expand — not because teams lack ambition, but because narrow scope makes it possible to learn quickly, demonstrate value, and build the organizational confidence needed for larger investments.

**Start with one workflow, not a platform.** Instead of "AI-powered customer support," start with "AI categorizes incoming support tickets by urgency and topic." Instead of "AI content generation," start with "AI drafts first versions of weekly team status updates." This tight scoping forces clarity about what exactly the AI is doing, who is using it, and how you will know if it is working.

**Define measurable success criteria before you build.** Without concrete metrics, AI projects tend to drift into vague assessments like "it seems helpful." Strong success criteria cover several angles. **Accuracy** establishes what percentage of outputs need to be correct and how you will measure correctness. **Speed** captures how much faster the AI-assisted workflow is compared to the manual one. **Adoption** sets a target for what percentage of users should be actively using the feature within a defined timeframe, such as 30 days. And **satisfaction** measures how users rate the quality of AI outputs, whether through a simple thumbs up/down mechanism or a short rating scale.

**Plan for the feedback loop.** The best AI features improve over time because they collect signal about what works and what doesn't. Design feedback mechanisms from the start so you can build on real usage data:
- Let users rate or correct AI outputs
- Log the inputs that produce poor results
- Track which categories of requests have the lowest accuracy
- Use this data to improve prompts, add examples, or refine guardrails

**Set a review cadence.** AI performance can drift as usage patterns change, new types of inputs appear, or the underlying task evolves. Plan monthly reviews of accuracy metrics, user feedback, and edge cases. This is how you catch degradation before users notice — and how you identify opportunities to expand the feature's scope based on demonstrated success.

sections.title: Evaluate a Use Case
sections.content: Apply the framework to a real scenario.

exercise.prompt:
A mid-size e-commerce company (200 employees, 50,000 orders/month) is considering three AI use cases:

1. **AI Product Descriptions** — Automatically generate product descriptions from product specs and images for their catalog of 5,000 products.

2. **AI Fraud Detection** — Use Claude to analyze order patterns and flag potentially fraudulent transactions before they're processed.

3. **AI Customer Email Drafts** — When a support agent opens a customer email, AI generates a draft response that the agent can edit and send.

For each use case, evaluate it using the five-dimension framework (Value, Feasibility, Risk, Data Readiness, User Acceptance). Give each dimension a score of 1-5 and explain your reasoning. Which use case would you recommend they start with, and why?
hints: Consider which tasks play to AI's strengths (language generation, classification) vs. weaknesses (deterministic accuracy, real-time data)
Think about what happens when the AI gets it wrong in each case — a bad product description vs. a missed fraud case vs. a tone-deaf email draft
Consider which use case has the fastest path to measurable impact
exercise.evaluationCriteria: Should evaluate all three use cases across all five dimensions. Strong responses will: (1) Rate AI Product Descriptions highly on feasibility and value (bulk content generation is an AI sweet spot) but note data readiness depends on having good specs; (2) Rate AI Fraud Detection low on feasibility (Claude isn't designed for pattern detection across numerical data — this is better suited to traditional ML) and high on risk (missing fraud is expensive); (3) Rate AI Customer Email Drafts highly on feasibility, value, and user acceptance (human-in-the-loop review mitigates risk). The recommendation should be either Product Descriptions or Email Drafts, with clear reasoning about the Fraud Detection case being a poor fit for LLMs. Bonus for discussing the human-in-the-loop pattern as a risk mitigation strategy.

sections.title: Key Takeaway
sections.content: The best AI use cases combine high value with high feasibility and manageable risk. Start by finding problems where language understanding or generation is the bottleneck, scope tightly to a single workflow, define measurable success criteria before building, and plan for continuous improvement through user feedback. Remember that a well-chosen narrow use case that delivers clear results will do more for your organization than an ambitious initiative that never quite proves its value.

sections.title: Check Your Understanding
sections.content: Reflect on how to apply the evaluation framework.

exercise.prompt: Your CEO just came back from a conference excited about AI and wants to "add AI to everything." They've asked you to identify the top AI opportunity across the company. Describe your approach: what questions would you ask, how would you prioritize opportunities, and how would you present your recommendation to get buy-in from both the CEO and the teams who would implement it?
exercise.evaluationCriteria: Should describe a structured approach: (1) Start by auditing current workflows for high-volume, repetitive language tasks — not jumping to solutions; (2) Interview teams to find pain points and time sinks; (3) Apply the evaluation framework to top candidates; (4) Recommend starting with one high-confidence use case rather than many; (5) Present with concrete metrics (time saved, error reduction) rather than vague AI hype; (6) Propose a pilot with clear success criteria and timeline. Bonus for mentioning the importance of choosing a use case with a visible win to build organizational confidence in AI.
